findings.problem: The chapter's hinge turn reads hand-timed rather than rule-timed. Aldric says he has been monitoring non-routine classification updates, then reveals Maren's bounty as if the crisis is arriving in the moment, but line 173 says the bounty has already been broadcasting for approximately four hours. The chapter never makes clear why Aldric withheld that information until the well, why Derry only gets or notices the alert in-scene, or why Fenhold still behaves as if nothing has changed before the reveal. Because the whole chapter pivots on this broadcast, the unclear timing and audience rules make the escalation feel author-convenient instead of causally legible.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite the reveal block at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:121, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:133, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:145, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:173 so the System event has an explicit sequence. Either make the bounty finalize on-page at the well, or explain in the text what Aldric was seeing before line 133, when the classification became live, who can actually receive the bounty notice, and why villagers like Breda have not already confronted Maren by the time he reaches the square. The repair needs to preserve the must-land beats of Derry noticing the change, the bounty's scale, and the decision to flee, but it cannot leave the chapter's central alert on ambiguous rules.
findings.acceptance_test: Reading snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:121-175 alone, a cold reader can state four concrete facts without inference gaps: when the bounty becomes active, why Aldric tells Maren at that moment rather than earlier, why Derry receives or reads the notice when he does, and why Fenhold has not already reacted publicly before the scene leaves the well. If any of those four answers still requires guesswork, this finding remains open.

findings.problem: Several of the chapter's strongest beats are followed by abstract paraphrase that explains what the scene has already made legible. After Derry's accusation, the narration translates his face into a full thesis about love, resentment, and fear. After Maren looks over Fenhold, the prose turns the moment into declared doctrine about the "comfortable lie" of blindness and home. At the village edge, the narration again explains the moral distinction between Derry's real life and Maren's fraudulent one instead of letting the departure image carry it. This repeated translation blunts pressure and slips toward emotional-summary prose the style bible explicitly forbids.
findings.rewrite_direction: Tighten or recast the explanatory passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:199-200, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:223-227, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:255-257. Keep the underlying dramatic work: Derry's mixed feeling, Maren's recognition that he is losing Fenhold, and the difference between Derry's rooted life and Maren's performed one. But deliver that work through image, gesture, silence, or a fresh concrete observation rather than abstract thematic paraphrase after the beat has already landed.
findings.acceptance_test: In the revised versions of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:199-200, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:223-227, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:255-257, the scene no longer names the full emotional equation in abstract summary after the decisive line or image. A reader should still be able to infer Derry's divided loyalty and Maren's fraud-versus-home realization from the revised text, but the passage must move forward on sensory detail or action instead of thesis language.

findings.problem: The chapter repeatedly falls back on the same explanatory comparison architecture: reactions and atmosphere are rendered through variations of "the way..." and "a man/woman who..." person-similes. Once or twice this is a choice; at this density it becomes visible machinery. Derry memorizes the room "the way a man" leaving would, Breda watches "with the attention of a woman who" has watched for years, Derry's face becomes "a man who" has finished crying, and so on. The recurrence flattens otherwise sharp observation into a reusable template, which the style bible explicitly warns against.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the repeated comparison scaffolds at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:79, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:97, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:179, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:199, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:233, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:247. Keep whichever one comparison is strongest, but recast the others into different sentence shapes or direct physical detail so each beat lands in its own syntax rather than through interchangeable analogies.
findings.acceptance_test: Across the revised passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:79, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:97, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:179, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:199, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:233, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:247, no more than one emotional beat may still rely on the chapter's current "a man/woman who..." explanatory template. The remaining beats must land through distinct images, gestures, or sentence rhythms that are visibly not copies of one another.

summary: The chapter clearly lands Derry noticing Maren's changed behavior, the bounty's scale, Derry's family cost, and the decision to flee, and the dialogue remains voice-distinct and style-bible-consistent. It still fails overall because the chapter's central plot turn depends on unclear bounty timing and visibility rules, and because the prose too often explains meanings the scene has already earned while leaning on a repeated comparison template that starts to read generated rather than controlled.
